feed.title: Sandor Dargo's Blog
feed.subtitle: Sandor Dargo writes about about C++, software development, books and stoic philosophy applied to software craftsmanship

entry.title: C++26: Delete with a reason
entry.summary: Let’s start exploring C++26 with a simple but useful change. Thanks to Yihe Li’s proposal (2573R2), when we =delete a special member function or a function overload, we can specify a reason. This is a practical and not very surprising change. Why do I say it’s not very surprising? Adding the ability to specify reasons for some operations has become a pattern in C++ evolution. Since C++11, we c...

entry.title: Let's start exploring C++26
entry.summary: During the last 2 years, we spent a lot of time exploring C++23 resulting in almost 40 blog posts. I’m not saying that we covered every single new language or library feature, but we covered most of them. Finally, C++23 became the official standard. We’re going to move our focus and over the next months or even years, we’ll start exploring C++26, the new version in bake. The scope of C++26 is ...

entry.title: Replace CRTP with concepts?
entry.summary: In my Meeting C++ 2024 trip report, among my favourite ideas I mentioned Klaus Iglberger’s talk where he mentioned the possibility of replacing the curiously returning template pattern with the help of class tagging and concepts. Class tagging might mean different things in different contexts, or at least might be implemented in different ways. The end goal is to mark, in other words, tag c...

entry.title: How to ensure a class is not copyable or movable

link.title: How to ensure a class is not copyable or movable

entry.summary: The topic of this post is to show different ways to ensure that a class is either non-moveable or non-copyable. But first of all, why would you need that? If we follow the classification proposed by Sebastian Theophil, we can talk about 4 different class types: value classes container classes resource classes singleton classes While the first two should be regular classes offering...

entry.title: Trip report: Meeting C++ 2024
entry.summary: Last week, I got the chance to go to Berlin and participate in the 10th edition of Meeting C++ which is as far as I know the biggest C++ conference in Europe. Considering both the online and onsite participants, there were about 500 of us, eager to learn more mostly but not only about C++. The conference was held in the East side of Berlin, in the Vienna House by Wyndham Andel’s Berlin Hotel o...
